steps.title: When Did It Happen?
steps.desc:
A2 begins. You can say what things ARE. Now you'll say what things WERE.

First: the time words. Without these, past tense has no anchor.

No more romanisation from this unit forward. You can read 한글. Trust yourself.
goals: Learn 8 core time words: 어제/오늘/내일 + week/year sets
Understand 지난/다음 as modifiers
See Sino-Korean time compounds
Prepare the vocabulary for past tense (Lesson 2)

steps.note:
어제: yesterday.

어제 is pure Korean (not Sino-Korean).
One of the most common words in daily conversation.

어제 뭐 했어요? = Yesterday what did-you-do?
SOV: time word first, verb last.

오늘: today.

Also pure Korean.
오늘 뭐 해요? = What are you doing today?

Time words in Korean go at the START of the sentence.
English: 'I ate yesterday' (time at end)
Korean: 어제 먹었어요 (time at start)

내일: tomorrow.

Pure Korean word.
내일 뭐 해요? = What are you doing tomorrow?
내일 만나요! = See you tomorrow!

어제 → 오늘 → 내일
yesterday → today → tomorrow

steps.exampleEn: I saw a movie last week.
steps.note:
COMPOUND: 지난 (passed/last) + 주 (week).
지나다: to pass by.
주 (週): week.

지난 = modifier meaning 'last/previous'.
This 지난 works with many time nouns:
지난주 (last week), 지난달 (last month).

COMPOUND: 다음 (next) + 주 (week).
다음: next/following.

다음 works like 지난:
다음 주 (next week)
다음 달 (next month)

지난 ↔ 다음: last ↔ next

steps.exampleEn: I went to Korea last year.
steps.note:
SINO-KOREAN: 작 (昨: last/previous) + 년 (年: year).

작 appears in: 작년 (last year), 작일 (yesterday — literary).
년 appears in: 매년 (every year), 내년, 올해.

작년에: last year (에 for time point).

steps.exampleEn: I'm studying Korean hard this year.
steps.note:
올해: this year.
올 (this/current) + 해 (year — pure Korean).

해 = year (pure Korean). 년 = year (Sino-Korean).
Both mean year, different registers:
올해 (conversational) vs 금년 (formal/Sino-Korean).

steps.exampleEn: I want to go to Japan next year.
steps.note:
SINO-KOREAN: 내 (來: coming) + 년 (年: year).

내 (來) = coming/next.
Same 내 as in 내일 (tomorrow: coming + day).

작년 → 올해 → 내년
last year → this year → next year

steps.title: 📅 Time Word Pairs
steps.text:
Korean time words come in clean pairs:

어제 ↔ 내일 (yesterday ↔ tomorrow)
지난주 ↔ 다음 주 (last week ↔ next week)
지난달 ↔ 다음 달 (last month ↔ next month)
작년 ↔ 내년 (last year ↔ next year)

Center: 오늘 (today), 이번 주 (this week), 올해 (this year)

All past time words use 에:
어제 — no 에 needed (standalone)
지난주에, 작년에 — need 에
오늘, 내일 — usually no 에

deepDive.title: 이번 — This/Current
deepDive.text:
이번: this (current cycle).
이번 주: this week
이번 달: this month
이번 학기: this semester

이번 also means 'this time':
이번에는 잘 할 거예요.
= This time I'll do well.

이 (this) + 번 (turn/time).
번 (番): turn, number.
1번: number 1.
이번: this turn.

steps.hint: 지난 = passed/last. 주 = week.
내 (來) = coming. Same as 내일 (tomorrow: coming + day).
작 (昨) = last/previous. 작년 = last year.

steps.q: Where do time words usually go in a Korean sentence?
steps.hint: Korean: 어제 영화를 봤어요 (yesterday movie saw). Time first.
해 = year (pure Korean). 년 = year (Sino-Korean). Both mean year.

steps.s: ___ 뭐 했어요? (What did you do yesterday?)
steps.hint: Yesterday = 어제

steps.s: ___에 한국에 가고 싶어요. (I want to go to Korea next year.)
steps.hint: Next year = 내 (coming) + 년 (year)

steps.s: ___에 영화를 봤어요. (I saw a movie last week.)
steps.hint: Last week = 지난 (passed) + 주 (week)

steps.s: {1} 한국에 갔어요. {2} 일본에 가고 싶어요.
steps.hint: Last year I went to Korea. Next year I want to go to Japan.

steps.q: 'I want to go to Japan next year' — correct word order:
steps.hint: Time first, then place, then verb (SOV).

steps.title: The Past Tense — Your First Tense Car
steps.desc:
You've been seeing past tense forms since Unit 6 Lesson 5.

먹었어요, 갔어요, 했어요 — you already know what they mean.

Now the system gets its name. Remember the Train?
Stem = engine. Endings = cars.
Today you add the FIRST TENSE CAR: past tense.

This is a P26 harvest. You've felt it. Now you'll own it.
goals: Understand stem + 았/었/했 + 어요 as the past tense formula
Apply the formula to verbs you already know
Recognize 합쇼체 past forms by ear
Produce past tense forms independently

steps.title: 🚂 The Train: Adding the Past Tense Car
steps.text:
Remember the Train from Unit 1?

STEM (engine) → [tense car] → [politeness car]

Present: 먹 + 어요 = 먹어요 (eat)
Past: 먹 + 었 + 어요 = 먹었어요 (ate)

The past tense car is: 았/었/했
It snaps between the stem and the politeness ending.

았: after bright vowels (ㅏ, ㅗ)
었: after dark vowels (everything else)
했: 하다 verbs (always)

This is the SAME vowel harmony rule from Unit 2 Lesson 2b.
You already know which vowels are bright and dark.

steps.note:
먹다: stem = 먹 (consonant ending).

먹 has no final vowel in the stem syllable.
Default to 었.
먹 + 었어요 = 먹었어요.

Consonant stems always get the full 았/었 attached.

가다: stem = 가.

가: bright vowel ㅏ → 았어요.
가 + 았어요 = 갔어요 (contraction: ㅏ + ㅏ merges).

Same contraction as present tense:
가 + 아요 = 가요 (present)
가 + 았어요 = 갔어요 (past)

steps.exampleEn: A friend came to my house.
steps.note:
오다: stem = 오.

오: bright vowel ㅗ → 았어요.
오 + 았어요 = 왔어요 (ㅗ + ㅏ = ㅘ contraction).

Same pattern as present:
오 + 아요 = 와요 (present)
오 + 았어요 = 왔어요 (past)

하다: ALWAYS → 했어요.

하다 verbs are the most common in Korean.
Every 하다 verb makes past tense the same way:

공부하다 → 공부했어요 (studied)
운동하다 → 운동했어요 (exercised)
요리하다 → 요리했어요 (cooked)
전화하다 → 전화했어요 (called)

하 + 았어요 = 했어요 (special contraction: ㅏ+ㅏ → ㅐ)

보다: stem = 보.

보: bright vowel ㅗ → 았어요.
보 + 았어요 = 봤어요 (ㅗ + ㅏ = ㅘ).

Identical contraction to 오다 → 왔어요.

마시다: stem = 마시.

시: dark vowel ㅣ → 었어요.
마시 + 었어요 = 마셨어요 (ㅣ + ㅓ = ㅕ contraction).

Same as present: 마시 + 어요 = 마셔요.
Past just adds 었: 마시 + 었어요 = 마셨어요.

steps.title: 📋 The Past Tense Formula
steps.text:
Three patterns. That's it.

① BRIGHT stem (ㅏ or ㅗ) → 았어요
가다 → 갔어요
오다 → 왔어요
보다 → 봤어요
살다 → 살았어요

② DARK stem (everything else) → 었어요
먹다 → 먹었어요
마시다 → 마셨어요
배우다 → 배웠어요
쓰다 → 썼어요

③ 하다 → 했어요 (always)
공부하다 → 공부했어요
운동하다 → 운동했어요

The contractions are the SAME as present tense.
If you know 가요, you know 갔어요.
The past just inserts 았/었/했 before -어요.

steps.hint: 먹: consonant stem, dark vowel context → 었어요
하다 → 했어요. Always.
보: bright ㅗ → 았어요. 보+았 contracts to 봤.
배우: dark ㅜ → 었어요. 우+었 contracts to 웠.

steps.q: The past tense 'car' in the Train metaphor is:
steps.hint: The past tense car snaps between stem and politeness ending.

steps.s: 어제 학교에 ___. (I went to school yesterday.)
steps.hint: 가다 → 가 + 았어요 = 갔어요

steps.s: 커피를 ___. (I drank coffee.)
steps.hint: 마시다 → 마시 + 었어요 = 마셨어요

steps.s: 어제 뭐 ___? (What did you do yesterday?)
steps.hint: 하다 → 했어요 (always)

steps.s: 어제 영화를 {1}. 팝콘을 {2}. 재미있었어요!
steps.hint: Yesterday I watched a movie. I ate popcorn. It was fun!

steps.title: 👂 합쇼체 Past — Just Recognize
steps.text:
You know 합쇼체 from Unit 3 — the formal speech level.
In announcements, news, and formal settings, past tense sounds different:

했어요 → 했습니다 (formal)
갔어요 → 갔습니다
먹었어요 → 먹었습니다

Pattern: stem + 았/었/했 + 습니다

You'll hear this on the news, in speeches, at airports.
⚡ JRTF: Just recognize it. Full 합쇼체 production in Unit 9.

deepDive.title: Why Two Speech Levels?
deepDive.text:
해요체 (polite casual): 했어요
합쇼체 (polite formal): 했습니다

Both are polite. The difference:
해요체: conversations, daily life, most situations
합쇼체: presentations, news, speeches, service industry

A barista says: 감사합니다 (합쇼체)
Your friend says: 고마워요 (해요체)

You're building 해요체 first because it covers 90% of daily life.

steps.q: 먹었습니다 is the ___ form of 먹었어요:
steps.hint: 합쇼체: -습니다/-ㅂ니다 endings. Formal but polite.
있다: dark vowel → 었어요. 재미있 + 었어요.

steps.title: Vowel Harmony — Applied to Past Tense
steps.desc:
You learned vowel harmony in Unit 2 Lesson 2b.

Bright vowels (ㅏ, ㅗ) attract 아.
Dark vowels (everything else) attract 어.
하다 always becomes 해.

That same rule now drives your past tense.
This lesson isn't teaching something new — it's confirming what you already feel.
goals: Apply vowel harmony to past tense systematically
Handle all contraction patterns in past tense
Distinguish bright/dark stem verbs instantly
Build past tense speed and confidence

steps.title: 🎵 The Rule You Already Know
steps.text:
Unit 2 Lesson 2b taught you:

BRIGHT (ㅏ, ㅗ) → 아요 → 았어요
DARK (all others) → 어요 → 었어요
하다 → 해요 → 했어요

Present and past use the SAME vowel harmony.
If you know the present form, you know the past.

가요 → 갔어요 (bright)
먹어요 → 먹었어요 (dark)
해요 → 했어요 (하다)

The contraction patterns are identical too.

steps.note:
살다: stem = 살.

Final vowel ㅏ = bright → 았어요.
살 + 았어요 = 살았어요.

No contraction needed — consonant stem.
살 is a consonant-ending stem, so 았 attaches directly.

읽다: stem = 읽.

Final vowel ㅣ = dark → 었어요.
읽 + 었어요 = 읽었어요.

Consonant stem: 었 attaches directly.

쓰다: stem = 쓰.

ㅡ drops before vowel endings (you saw this in Unit 6 L5).
쓰 + 었어요 → ㅡ drops → 썼어요.

Same as present: 쓰 + 어요 = 써요.
Past: 쓰 + 었어요 = 썼어요.

배우다: stem = 배우.

Final vowel ㅜ = dark → 었어요.
배우 + 었어요 = 배웠어요 (ㅜ+ㅓ = ㅝ contraction).

Same as present: 배우 + 어요 = 배워요.

만나다: stem = 만나.

Final vowel ㅏ = bright → 았어요.
만나 + 았어요 = 만났어요 (ㅏ+ㅏ merges).

Same contraction as 가다 → 갔어요.

주다: stem = 주.

Final vowel ㅜ = dark → 었어요.
주 + 었어요 = 줬어요 (ㅜ+ㅓ = ㅝ contraction).

Same pattern as 배우다.

steps.title: 📋 Complete Past Tense Contraction Map
steps.text:
VOWEL STEMS — contractions apply:
가 + 았어요 = 갔어요 (ㅏ+ㅏ merge)
오 + 았어요 = 왔어요 (ㅗ+ㅏ = ㅘ)
보 + 았어요 = 봤어요 (ㅗ+ㅏ = ㅘ)
마시 + 었어요 = 마셨어요 (ㅣ+ㅓ = ㅕ)
배우 + 었어요 = 배웠어요 (ㅜ+ㅓ = ㅝ)
쓰 + 었어요 = 썼어요 (ㅡ drops)
주 + 었어요 = 줬어요 (ㅜ+ㅓ = ㅝ)

CONSONANT STEMS — no contraction:
먹 + 었어요 = 먹었어요
읽 + 었어요 = 읽었어요
살 + 았어요 = 살았어요

하다 VERBS — always 했:
공부하다 → 공부했어요
요리하다 → 요리했어요

steps.q: 살다 (to live) — stem 살, vowel ㅏ. Past tense:
steps.hint: ㅏ = bright → 았어요. 살 + 았어요 = 살았어요.
ㅡ drops before vowel endings. 쓰 + 었어요 → 썼어요.
만나: ㅏ+ㅏ merge. 만나 + 았어요 = 만났어요.
주: ㅜ = dark → 었어요. ㅜ+ㅓ = ㅝ. 줬어요.

steps.q: Which rule determines 았 vs 었?
opts: Whether the verb is action or descriptive
Vowel harmony: bright ㅏ/ㅗ → 았, others → 었
Whether the subject is 1st or 3rd person
steps.ans: Vowel harmony: bright ㅏ/ㅗ → 았, others → 었
steps.hint: Same rule since Unit 2 L2b. Bright = 았, dark = 었.

steps.s: 어제 서울에서 ___. (I lived in Seoul.)
steps.hint: 살다: ㅏ = bright → 았어요

steps.s: 편지를 ___. (I wrote a letter.)
steps.hint: 쓰다: ㅡ drops → 썼어요

steps.s: 어제 친구를 ___. (I met a friend yesterday.)
steps.hint: 만나: ㅏ+ㅏ merge → 만났어요

steps.s: 어제 한국어를 {1}. 책을 {2}. 편지도 {3}.
steps.hint: Yesterday I learned Korean. I read a book. I also wrote a letter.
Same contraction. 오+았 = 왔. Present: 와요. Past: 왔어요.
하다 → 했어요. Always. 요리했어요.

steps.title: Talking About Yesterday
steps.desc:
Time to use everything: time words + past tense in real conversation.

You'll practice narrating your day, asking about others' days, and responding naturally.

Cultural anchor: 찜질방 — Korea's communal bathhouse culture.
goals: Ask and answer 어제 뭐 했어요?
Narrate a sequence of past events
Learn 찜질방 vocabulary and culture
Encounter two new grammar patterns naturally (just notice them)

steps.exampleEn: What did you do yesterday? — I met a friend.
steps.note:
어제 뭐 했어요?

어제: yesterday
뭐: what (shortened from 무엇을)
했어요: did (하다 past)

This is THE most common past tense question.
Answer: [time] + [object/place] + [verb past].

steps.exampleEn: I went to a jjimjilbang yesterday.
steps.note:
COMPOUND: 찜질 (hot therapy/heating) + 방 (room).
찜: steam/heat. 질: action/treatment. 방: room.

찜질방: a large communal bathhouse with saunas, pools, sleeping areas, snack bars.

Koreans go to 찜질방 with family and friends.
Open 24 hours. Some people sleep there.
Entry: ~10,000원–15,000원.

steps.exampleEn: I ate boiled eggs at the jjimjilbang.
steps.note:
삶은 계란: boiled egg.
삶다: to boil. 계란: egg (雞卵: chicken + egg).

찜질방 snacks:
삶은 계란: boiled eggs (brown, sweet)
식혜: sweet rice drink
바나나 우유: banana milk

These are ESSENTIAL 찜질방 experiences.

steps.title: ♨️ 찜질방 Culture
steps.text:
찜질방 is not just a bath. It's a social institution.

What happens at 찜질방:
1. 탈의실 (changing room) — everyone gets matching clothes
2. 목욕탕 (bathing area) — hot pools, cold pools, saunas
3. 찜질 (sauna rooms) — different temperatures, salt room, ice room
4. 공용 공간 (common area) — TV, snacks, sleeping mats
5. 식당 (restaurant) — full meals available

People go for:
• 스트레스 해소 (stress relief)
• Family bonding
• Birthday celebrations
• After-exam relaxation

양머리: the 'sheep head' towel — everyone makes it.
It's a rolled towel worn on the head to keep cool.

찜질방 = Korean 정 (community warmth) in action.

deepDive.title: 찜질방 Vocabulary
deepDive.text:
탈의실: changing room (탈의 = undress + 실 = room)
수건: towel
사우나: sauna
냉탕: cold bath (냉 = cold + 탕 = pool)
온탕: hot bath (온 = warm + 탕 = pool)
때밀이: body scrub service
안마: massage
수면실: sleeping room (수면 = sleep + 실 = room)

찜질방에서 뭐 했어요?
사우나에 들어갔어요. (I went into the sauna.)
삶은 계란을 먹었어요. (I ate boiled eggs.)
친구하고 이야기했어요. (I talked with a friend.)

steps.note:
재미있다 → past: 재미있었어요.

있다: dark vowel → 었어요.
재미있 + 었어요 = 재미있었어요.

재미없었어요: it wasn't fun (없다 → 없었어요).

steps.exampleEn: I went together with a friend.
steps.note:
같이: together.

친구하고 같이: together with a friend.
하고 = and/with (from Unit 3).

같이 가요! = Let's go together!
같이 먹어요! = Let's eat together!

steps.exampleEn: I was tired. So I slept early.
steps.note:
그래서: so, therefore.
You learned this connector in Unit 6.

Now use it to chain past events:
찜질방에 갔어요. 그래서 피곤했어요.
= I went to a jjimjilbang. So I was tired.

피곤하다: to be tired. 피곤했어요: was tired.
자다: to sleep. 잤어요: slept (자+았어요).

steps.hint: The question asks about yesterday — answer must be past tense.
방: room. 노래방 (singing room), 피시방 (PC room), 찜질방 (hot therapy room).

steps.q: 재미있었어요 is the past tense of:
steps.hint: 재미있다 → 재미있었어요 (있다: dark → 었어요)
자: bright ㅏ → 았어요. 자+았 = 잤어요.

steps.q: Complete the narration: 찜질방에 갔어요. ___ 삶은 계란을 먹었어요.
steps.hint: Cause and result: went to jjimjilbang, SO ate boiled eggs.

steps.s: 친구하고 ___ 영화를 봤어요. (I watched a movie together with a friend.)
steps.hint: 같이 = together

steps.s: 피곤했어요. ___ 일찍 잤어요. (I was tired. So I slept early.)
steps.hint: Cause → result = 그래서

steps.s: 찜질방에서 삶은 ___을 먹었어요.
steps.hint: 삶은 계란 = boiled egg — classic 찜질방 snack.

steps.s: 어제 친구하고 {1} 찜질방에 {2}. 삶은 계란을 {3}. {4}!
steps.hint: Yesterday I went to the jjimjilbang with a friend. I ate boiled eggs. It was fun!

steps.title: 👀 Notice These Forms — JRTF
steps.text:
In natural Korean conversation, you'll hear:

와, 찜질방이 좋네요!
→ Wow, the jjimjilbang is nice! (-네요: surprise/discovery)

한국에 오게 됐어요.
→ I came to be in Korea. / It happened that I came to Korea.
(-게 되다: 'it came to be that...')

⚡ JRTF: Don't analyze these yet.
-네요: formal name in Unit 13.
-게 되다: formal name in Unit 15.
Just notice them. Let them feel natural first.

deepDive.title: Why JRTF?
deepDive.text:
LingoVerse teaches grammar the way polyglots acquire it:

1. EXPOSURE — you hear/see the form in context
2. PATTERN FEEL — your brain starts recognizing it
3. INSTINCT — it starts feeling 'right' before you know why
4. NAMING — you get the formal label (confirmation)
5. GENERATION — you produce it yourself

-네요 and -게 되다 are at step 1 right now.
By the time they get named (Units 13 and 15), you'll already feel them.

opts: A communal bathhouse with saunas and sleeping areas
steps.ans: A communal bathhouse with saunas and sleeping areas
steps.hint: 찜질방 = steam/heat room — bathhouse, saunas, common areas, food, sleeping.

steps.title: The Irregular Families — ㄷ and ㅂ
steps.desc:
You've been seeing these since Unit 6 Lesson 5.

더워요, 추워요, 어려워요 — you know these forms.
들어요, 걸어요 — these too.

They're not random. They're two families with one rule each.
Today those families get their names.
goals: Name the ㄷ-irregular family and its rule
Name the ㅂ-irregular family and its rule
Apply both rules to past tense
Know which common verbs belong to each family

steps.title: ⚡ You've Seen These Since Unit 6
steps.text:
Unit 6 Lesson 5 showed you:

덥다 → 더워요 (hot)
춥다 → 추워요 (cold)
어렵다 → 어려워요 (difficult)

듣다 → 들어요 (listen)
걷다 → 걸어요 (walk)

You used JRTF: 'just remember this form.'
Now the pattern gets its name.

These are IRREGULAR VERBS — verbs whose stems change before vowel endings.
Most Korean verbs are perfectly regular.
These two families are the most common exceptions.

steps.note:
THE RULE: When a ㄷ-irregular stem meets a vowel ending, ㄷ changes to ㄹ.

듣다: stem = 듣
듣 + 어요 → ㄷ→ㄹ → 들 + 어요 = 들어요
듣 + 었어요 → 들 + 었어요 = 들었어요

Why? Historical sound change.
In old Korean, ㄷ softened to ㄹ between vowels.
The change stuck for some verbs.

걷다: ㄷ-irregular.
걷 + 어요 → 걸어요
걷 + 었어요 → 걸었어요

어제 공원에서 많이 걸었어요.
= I walked a lot in the park yesterday.

많이: a lot. 공원: park (公園).

묻다: ㄷ-irregular.
묻 + 어요 → 물어요
묻 + 었어요 → 물었어요

⚠️ 묻다 has TWO meanings:
묻다 (to ask): ㄷ-IRREGULAR → 물어요
묻다 (to bury): REGULAR → 묻어요

Context always makes it clear.

steps.title: 📋 ㄷ-Irregular Family
steps.text:
Common ㄷ-irregular verbs:

듣다 (listen) → 들어요 / 들었어요
걷다 (walk) → 걸어요 / 걸었어요
묻다 (ask) → 물어요 / 물었어요
싣다 (load) → 실어요 / 실었어요
깨닫다 (realize) → 깨달아요 / 깨달았어요

NOT ㄷ-irregular (regular):
받다 (receive) → 받아요 / 받았어요 ✓ regular
닫다 (close) → 닫아요 / 닫았어요 ✓ regular

How to know? Frequency. The irregular ones are the COMMON daily verbs: listen, walk, ask. The regular ones tend to be less ambiguous.

steps.exampleEn: to be hot → it's hot / it was hot
steps.note:
THE RULE: When a ㅂ-irregular stem meets a vowel ending, ㅂ changes to 우.

덥다: stem = 덥
덥 + 어요 → ㅂ→우 → 더우 + 어요 = 더워요 (우+어 = 워)
덥 + 었어요 → 더우 + 었어요 = 더웠어요

The ㅂ becomes 우, then the usual contractions apply.
우 + 어 = 워 (you know this contraction from 배우다 → 배워요).

steps.en: cold → it's cold / it was cold
steps.note:
춥다: ㅂ-irregular.
춥 + 어요 → 추우 + 어요 = 추워요
춥 + 었어요 → 추우 + 었어요 = 추웠어요

Same pattern as 덥다.

steps.en: difficult → it's difficult / it was difficult
steps.note:
어렵다: ㅂ-irregular.
어렵 + 어요 → 어려우 + 어요 = 어려워요
어렵 + 었어요 → 어려우 + 었어요 = 어려웠어요

시험 (試驗): exam. 시 (test) + 험 (examine).

steps.en: easy → it's easy / it was easy
steps.note:
쉽다: ㅂ-irregular.
쉽 + 어요 → 쉬우 + 어요 = 쉬워요
쉽 + 었어요 → 쉬우 + 었어요 = 쉬웠어요

어렵다 ↔ 쉽다: difficult ↔ easy.
Both ㅂ-irregular!

steps.title: 📋 ㅂ-Irregular Family
steps.text:
Common ㅂ-irregular verbs:

덥다 (hot) → 더워요 / 더웠어요
춥다 (cold) → 추워요 / 추웠어요
어렵다 (difficult) → 어려워요 / 어려웠어요
쉽다 (easy) → 쉬워요 / 쉬웠어요
무겁다 (heavy) → 무거워요 / 무거웠어요
가볍다 (light) → 가벼워요 / 가벼웠어요
아름답다 (beautiful) → 아름다워요 / 아름다웠어요
바쁘다 — ✓ REGULAR! (바빠요/바빴어요)

⚠️ 바쁘다 is NOT ㅂ-irregular.
It's a ㅡ-stem verb. 바쁘 + 아요 → 바빠요.
The ㅂ is part of the syllable, not the final consonant.

NOT ㅂ-irregular (regular):
잡다 (catch) → 잡아요 ✓ regular
입다 (wear) → 입어요 ✓ regular

steps.hint: ㄷ-irregular: ㄷ→ㄹ before vowels. 듣→들+었어요.
ㅂ-irregular: ㅂ→우. 덥→더우+었어요 = 더웠어요.
받다 is REGULAR. ㅏ = bright → 았어요. 받+았어요.
ㄷ-irregular: 걷→걸. 걸+었어요 = 걸었어요.
바쁘다 is a ㅡ-stem verb, not ㅂ-irregular. 바쁘+아요 = 바빠요.
ㅂ→우: 어렵→어려우+었어요 = 어려웠어요.

steps.s: 어제 음악을 ___. (I listened to music yesterday.)
steps.hint: 듣다: ㄷ-irregular → 들+었어요

steps.s: 시험이 ___. (The exam was difficult.)
steps.hint: 어렵다: ㅂ-irregular → 어려우+었어요

steps.s: 지난주에 많이 ___. (I walked a lot last week.)
steps.hint: 걷다: ㄷ-irregular → 걸+었어요

steps.s: 어제 날씨가 {1}. 공원에서 많이 {2}. 음악을 {3}.
steps.hint: Yesterday the weather was hot. I walked a lot in the park. I listened to music.
ㅂ→우: 무겁→무거우+었어요 = 무거웠어요.

steps.title: Saying What You Didn't Do
steps.desc:
You know 안 (won't/don't) and 못 (can't) from Unit 6.
Now apply them to past tense.

안 먹었어요 = didn't eat (chose not to)
못 먹었어요 = couldn't eat (was unable)

Plus the long forms: -지 않았어요, -지 못했어요.
goals: Use 안 + past verb for negative past
Use 못 + past verb for inability past
Use -지 않았어요 long form
Use -지 못했어요 long form
Maintain the 안 vs 못 distinction in past tense

steps.note:
안 + [verb past] = chose not to / didn't.

안 먹었어요: didn't eat
안 갔어요: didn't go
안 했어요: didn't do

Same position as present tense:
안 먹어요 (don't eat) → 안 먹었어요 (didn't eat)

안 goes directly before the verb.

못 + [verb past] = couldn't / was unable.

못 갔어요: couldn't go
못 먹었어요: couldn't eat
못 했어요: couldn't do

Remember Unit 6:
안 = choice (I chose not to)
못 = inability (I couldn't)

This distinction stays the same in past tense.

-지 않았어요: long form of negative past.

Formula: stem + 지 않았어요.

공부하다 → 공부하지 않았어요
먹다 → 먹지 않았어요
가다 → 가지 않았어요

This is the same -지 않다 pattern from A1, now in past tense.
않다 → 않았어요 (past of 않다).

-지 못했어요: long form of inability past.

Formula: stem + 지 못했어요.

가다 → 가지 못했어요 (couldn't go)
먹다 → 먹지 못했어요 (couldn't eat)
하다 → 하지 못했어요 (couldn't do)

못하다 → 못했어요 (past of 못하다).

steps.title: 📋 Negative Past — Complete Map
steps.text:
SHORT FORMS:
안 + [verb past] = chose not to
못 + [verb past] = was unable to

LONG FORMS:
stem + 지 않았어요 = did not
stem + 지 못했어요 = could not

Examples:
안 먹었어요 = 먹지 않았어요 (didn't eat)
못 먹었어요 = 먹지 못했어요 (couldn't eat)

Short forms: casual, spoken Korean.
Long forms: slightly more formal, written Korean.
Meaning is identical within each pair.

steps.hint: 안 = by choice. 안 + past = 안 갔어요.
못 = unable. 못 + past = 못 갔어요.
Long form: stem + 지 않았어요. 공부하 + 지 않았어요.

steps.q: 아파서 학교에 ___. (I was sick so I couldn't go to school.)
steps.hint: Sick = unable (못). Not a choice.

steps.q: 배가 안 고파서 ___. (I wasn't hungry so I didn't eat.)
steps.hint: Not hungry = choice not to eat (안).
Long form -지 않았어요 = short form 안 + past.

steps.s: 어제 비가 와서 ___ 갔어요. (It rained yesterday so I couldn't go.)
steps.hint: Rain prevented going = inability = 못.

steps.s: 시간이 없어서 공부___ 못했어요. (I didn't have time so I couldn't study.)
steps.hint: Long form: stem + 지 못했어요. 공부하다 → 공부하지 못했어요.

steps.s: 어제 ___ 먹었어요. 배가 안 고팠어요. (I didn't eat yesterday. I wasn't hungry.)
steps.hint: Not hungry = chose not to eat = 안.

steps.s: 어제 비가 와서 학교에 {1} 갔어요. 집에서 공부{2} 않았어요. 텔레비전을 {3}.
steps.hint: It rained so I couldn't go to school. I didn't study at home. I watched TV.

steps.q: Which pair has the same meaning?
steps.hint: Short 안 + past = long -지 않았어요. Same meaning, different form.

steps.q: 다리를 다쳐서 ___ 걸었어요. (I hurt my leg so I couldn't walk.)
steps.hint: Injury = inability = 못.

steps.title: 💡 Common Pattern: 아/어서 + 못
steps.text:
Korean often explains WHY you couldn't do something:

비가 와서 못 갔어요.
= It rained so I couldn't go.

바빠서 못 만났어요.
= I was busy so I couldn't meet (them).

아파서 못 왔어요.
= I was sick so I couldn't come.

Pattern: [reason]-아/어서 + 못 + [past verb].

-아/어서 = because/so (you've seen this since Unit 6).
This is the most natural way to explain inability in Korean.

steps.title: Weekend Diary — Connecting Past Events
steps.desc:
You can say what you did. Now: connect events into a narrative.

Two connectors for sequencing:
-고: and (then)
-아/어서: because/and so

These let you tell stories, not just list events.
goals: Use -고 to chain events in sequence
Use -아/어서 for cause and sequence
Write a short narration of past events
Combine all past tense tools into natural paragraphs

steps.exampleEn: I ate rice and (then) drank coffee.
steps.note:
-고: connector meaning 'and/and then'.

Attach to the STEM of the first verb:
먹다 → 먹 + 고 = 먹고
가다 → 가 + 고 = 가고
하다 → 하 + 고 = 하고

-고 is the simplest Korean connector.
No vowel harmony. No contraction. Just stem + 고.

밥을 먹고 커피를 마셨어요.
= (I) ate rice and drank coffee.

steps.exampleEn: I ate breakfast so I felt good.
steps.note:
-아/어서: because, and so, and then.

You've seen this in Unit 6 as a connector.
Two uses:

① Cause: 비가 와서 못 갔어요. (It rained so I couldn't go.)
② Sequence: 일어나서 밥을 먹었어요. (I woke up and then ate.)

Attaches with vowel harmony:
가 + 아서 = 가서
먹 + 어서 = 먹어서
하 + 여서 = 해서

⚠️ When -아/어서 is used, ONLY the last verb gets tense:
일어나서 밥을 먹었어요. ✓
일어났서 밥을 먹었어요. ✗

steps.title: 📋 -고 vs -아/어서
steps.text:
-고: simple listing, no cause implied.
밥을 먹고 커피를 마셨어요.
= I ate and (also) drank coffee. (two facts)

-아/어서: cause or natural sequence.
일어나서 밥을 먹었어요.
= I woke up and (then) ate. (one led to the next)

배가 고파서 밥을 먹었어요.
= I was hungry so I ate. (cause → result)

Key difference:
-고: tense can appear on first verb OR both.
-아/어서: tense ONLY on the final verb.

밥을 먹고 커피를 마셨어요. ✓
밥을 먹었고 커피를 마셨어요. ✓ (emphasizes both are past)

일어나서 밥을 먹었어요. ✓
일어났어서 밥을 먹었어요. ✗ (WRONG)

steps.en: to wake up/get up → woke up
steps.exampleEn: I woke up early in the morning.
steps.note:
일어나다: to get up/wake up.

일어나: bright ㅏ → 았어요.
일어나 + 았어요 = 일어났어요.

아침에: in the morning.
일찍: early.

아침에 일찍 일어났어요.
= I woke up early in the morning.

운동하다: to exercise.
운동 (運動): movement/exercise.
운 (carry/move) + 동 (move).

하다 → 했어요.
운동했어요 = exercised.

쉬다: to rest.
쉬: dark ㅟ → 었어요.
쉬 + 었어요 = 쉬었어요.

집에서 쉬었어요 = I rested at home.

피곤해서 쉬었어요.
= I was tired so I rested.

청소하다: to clean.
청소 (清掃): cleaning.
청 (clean/clear) + 소 (sweep).

방: room.
방을 청소했어요 = I cleaned my room.

하다 → 했어요. 청소했어요.

steps.title: 📝 Sample Weekend Diary
steps.text:
주말에 뭐 했어요?

토요일에 일찍 일어나서 운동했어요.
공원에서 많이 걸었어요.
오후에 친구를 만나서 같이 영화를 봤어요.
재미있었어요!

일요일에 집에서 쉬었어요.
방을 청소하고 한국어를 공부했어요.
저녁에 가족하고 밥을 먹었어요.

번역:
What did you do on the weekend?
Saturday I woke up early and exercised.
I walked a lot in the park.
In the afternoon I met a friend and watched a movie together.
It was fun!
Sunday I rested at home.
I cleaned my room and studied Korean.
In the evening I ate with my family.

deepDive.title: 주말 (Weekend)
deepDive.text:
주말: weekend.
주 (週) = week. 말 (末) = end.
Week-end = weekend.

토요일: Saturday. 일요일: Sunday.

평일: weekday (平日: flat/normal + day).

주말에 뭐 해요?
= What do you do on weekends?

주말에 뭐 했어요?
= What did you do last weekend?

steps.q: 밥을 먹___ 커피를 마셨어요. (I ate and then drank coffee.)
steps.hint: -고: and/and then. Simple sequence.

steps.q: 배가 고파___ 밥을 먹었어요. (I was hungry so I ate.)
steps.hint: -아/어서: because. 고프(다) + 아서 = 고파서.
With -아/어서, tense only on the LAST verb.
말 (末) = end. 주말 = week-end = weekend.
-아/어서: tense ONLY on the last verb. 일어났어서 is wrong.

steps.s: 일찍 일어나___ 운동했어요. (I woke up early and exercised.)
steps.hint: Natural sequence: woke up → exercised. -아/어서.

steps.s: 방을 청소___  한국어를 공부했어요. (I cleaned my room and studied Korean.)
steps.hint: -고: and/and then. Two parallel activities.

steps.s: 피곤___  집에서 쉬었어요. (I was tired so I rested at home.)
steps.hint: Cause: tired → rested. 피곤하다 + 아/어서 = 피곤해서.

steps.s: 토요일에 일찍 {1} 공원에서 {2}. 오후에 친구를 만나{3} 영화를 {4}.
steps.hint: Saturday: woke up early, exercised in the park. Afternoon: met a friend and watched a movie.

steps.q: 저녁에 가족하고 밥을 먹었어요. 하고 here means:
steps.hint: 하고 = and/with. 가족하고 = with family.

steps.title: Verbs Becoming Adjectives — A Preview
steps.desc:
Something strange and beautiful in Korean:
Verbs can become adjectives.

먹다 (to eat) → 먹는 (eating / that one eats)
음식: food
먹는 음식: food that (I) eat

This lesson is just a preview. Don't memorize the system.
Just notice how Korean turns verbs into descriptions.
goals: See -는 attached to verb stems before nouns
Understand '먹는 음식' = 'food that (I) eat'
Notice how Korean relative clauses work
Build recognition for the full system in Unit 18

steps.exampleEn: What food do you eat every day?
steps.note:
먹는 음식:
먹다 → 먹 + 는 = 먹는 (eating / that one eats)
음식: food

먹는 음식 = food that one eats.

In English: 'the food that I eat'
In Korean: '먹는 음식' — the verb comes BEFORE the noun.
No 'that'. The verb just becomes an adjective.

⚡ JRTF: Full modifier system in Unit 18.
For now: just notice the pattern.

steps.exampleEn: Where is the place you live?
steps.note:
사는 곳:
살다 → 사 + 는 = 사는 (living / where one lives)
곳: place

사는 곳 = place where (you) live.

⚠️ 살다 → 사는 (not 살는).
When a stem ends in ㄹ, the ㄹ drops before -는.
This is a rule you'll learn fully in Unit 18.

좋아하는 것:
좋아하다 → 좋아하 + 는 = 좋아하는
것: thing

좋아하는 것 = thing that (you) like.

것 is extremely common:
좋아하는 것: what you like
싫어하는 것: what you dislike
먹고 싶은 것: what you want to eat

steps.exampleEn: Is there a café you often go to?
steps.note:
자주 가는 카페:
가다 → 가 + 는 = 가는
자주: often
카페: café

자주 가는 카페 = café that (you) often go to.

The whole phrase '자주 가는' works as one big adjective before 카페.

steps.title: 🔍 How This Works (Preview Only)
steps.text:
In English, relative clauses come AFTER the noun:
'the food THAT I eat'
'the place WHERE I live'
'the café THAT I often go to'

In Korean, they come BEFORE the noun:
'먹는 음식' = eat-ing food
'사는 곳' = live-ing place
'자주 가는 카페' = often go-ing café

The -는 ending turns a present-tense verb into a modifier.

There are also past and future modifier forms:
Past: -은/ㄴ (먹은 음식: food that I ate)
Future: -을/ㄹ (먹을 음식: food to eat)

⚡ JRTF: Full system in Unit 18.
For now: recognize -는 before nouns = 'that does...'

deepDive.title: Why This Matters
deepDive.text:
Korean doesn't have relative pronouns (who, which, that).
Instead, verbs transform directly into adjectives.

This means Korean relative clauses can be very compact:

제가 어제 친구하고 같이 먹은 비빔밥
= the bibimbap that I ate together with a friend yesterday

All of that comes BEFORE 비빔밥.

This is one of the most important structures in Korean.
Every advanced sentence uses it.
Unit 18 will build it step by step.
For now: just notice -는 before nouns.

steps.hint: 먹는 = eating/that eats. 음식 = food. = food that one eats.
사는 = living/where one lives. 곳 = place.

steps.q: In 좋아하는 것, -는 makes the verb into:
steps.hint: -는 turns present-tense verbs into modifiers before nouns.

steps.q: In Korean, the modifier clause goes ___ the noun:
steps.hint: Korean: [modifier] + noun. English: noun + [relative clause].
자주 = often. (You learned this in Unit 6.)

steps.s: 매일 ___ 음식이 뭐예요? (What food do you eat every day?)
steps.hint: Present modifier: stem + 는. 먹다 → 먹는.

steps.s: ___ 곳이 어디예요? (Where do you live?)
steps.hint: 살다 → 사는 (ㄹ drops before 는). Place where you live.

steps.s: 좋아___ 것이 뭐예요? (What do you like?)
steps.hint: 좋아하다 → 좋아하는. Present modifier.

steps.s: 매일 {1} 음식은 김치예요. {2} 곳은 서울이에요.
steps.hint: The food I eat every day is kimchi. The place I live is Seoul.

steps.q: This lesson is a ___ for Unit 18:
steps.hint: ⚡ JRTF: Just notice the pattern. Full system in Unit 18.

steps.title: 🧠 What to Take Away
steps.text:
You don't need to produce these yet.

Just recognize:
• -는 before a noun = present modifier
• The verb becomes an adjective
• Korean puts the description BEFORE the noun

먹는 음식, 사는 곳, 좋아하는 것

You'll see these in every Korean conversation.
By Unit 18, you'll be building them yourself.
For now: just notice them.

steps.title: Unit 7 Complete — A2 Has Begun
steps.desc:
You've unlocked:

• Past tense (았/었/했어요)
• Time words (어제, 지난주, 작년...)
• Vowel harmony applied to past tense
• ㄷ and ㅂ irregular families — named
• Negative past (안/못 + past)
• Sequence connectors (-고, -아/어서)
• Modifier preview (-는 + noun)

This quiz tests it all.
goals: Verify past tense formation mastery
Test irregular verb knowledge
Confirm negative past understanding
Demonstrate narrative ability

steps.hint: 가: bright ㅏ → 았어요. 가+았 = 갔어요.
마시: dark ㅣ → 었어요. ㅣ+ㅓ = ㅕ. 마셨어요.
ㄷ-irregular: 듣→들 before vowels. 들+었어요.
ㅂ-irregular: 쉽→쉬우. 쉬우+었어요 = 쉬웠어요.

steps.q: 'I couldn't eat because I was sick':
steps.hint: Sick = inability = 못. Cause = -아/어서.

steps.q: 일어나서 밥을 먹었어요. The -서 here expresses:
steps.hint: -아/어서: and then / because. Here: woke up THEN ate.

steps.q: 먹는 음식 — -는 makes the verb into:
steps.hint: -는: turns present verb into modifier before a noun.

steps.q: 작년 = 작 + 년. The correct meaning:
steps.hint: 작 (昨) = last/previous. 년 = year.

steps.q: 내년에 한국에 가고 싶어요. 내년 means:
steps.hint: 내 (來: coming) + 년 (year) = next year.
ㅡ drops: 쓰+었어요 → 썼어요.

steps.s: 어제 공원에서 많이 ___. (I walked a lot in the park yesterday.)
steps.hint: ㄷ-irregular: 걷→걸. 걸+었어요.

steps.s: 지난주에 날씨가 ___. (The weather was cold last week.)
steps.hint: ㅂ-irregular: 춥→추우. 추우+었어요 = 추웠어요.

steps.s: 배가 안 고파서 ___ 먹었어요. (I wasn't hungry so I didn't eat.)
steps.hint: Not hungry = choice = 안.

steps.s: 아침에 일찍 일어나___ 운동했어요.
steps.hint: Sequence: woke up then exercised. -아/어서.

steps.s: 어제 친구를 {1}. 같이 카페에 {2}. 커피를 {3}. {4}!
steps.hint: Yesterday I met a friend. We went to a café together. We drank coffee. It was fun!

steps.s: 토요일에 찜질방에 {1}. 삶은 계란을 {2}. 일요일에 집에서 {3}.
steps.hint: Saturday went to jjimjilbang. Ate boiled eggs. Sunday rested at home.

steps.title: 🎉 Unit 7 Complete
steps.text:
What you can do now:

✅ Talk about the past (았/었/했어요)
✅ Use time words to anchor events
✅ Handle ㄷ and ㅂ irregular verbs
✅ Negate in the past (안/못 + past)
✅ Chain events with -고 and -아/어서
✅ Recognize verb modifiers (먹는 음식)

Coming in Unit 8:
건강 (health) vocabulary
Conditional: -(으)면 (if)
-아/어야 하다 (must/have to)
Cultural anchor: Korean healthcare system

You're building real Korean now. 화이팅!

steps.q: You've completed Unit 7. Your past tense formula is:
steps.hint: Past = stem + [tense car: 았/었/했] + 어요.
